source_paper_title: Binary Extended Formulations and Sequential Convexification
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Aprile_2024, title={Binary Extended Formulations and Sequential Convexification}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0129}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0129}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Aprile, Manuel and Conforti, Michele and Di Summa, Marco}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1566–1581} }
context:
Section 5 (Conclusion), page 17.

In light of Lemmas 4.11, 4.13, one concludes that the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) is better than the full binarization \(B_F(d)\) in terms of rank. We do not know whether \(B_U(d)\) is optimal with respect to all binarizations that are isomorphic to a \(d\)-simplex. We leave this as our first open question.
title: Optimal rank among simplex-isomorphic binarizations for bounded integer variables
background_latex:
Let \(x\) be an integer variable taking values in \(\{0,1,\dots,k\}\). A (polyhedral) binarization of \(x\) is a polytope \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) in variables \((x,y)\), where \(y\in[0,1]^d\) are intended to be binary, such that
\[
\pi_x\bigl(\{(x,y)\in B: y\in\{0,1\}^d\}\bigr)=\{0,1,\dots,k\},
\]
where \(\pi_x\) denotes projection onto the \(x\)-coordinate.
A binarization \(B\) is called natural if for every vertex \((\bar x,\bar y)\in V(B)\) one has \(\bar x\in\mathbb{Z}\) (equivalently \(\pi_x(V(B))=\{0,1,\dots,k\}\)).

Given a mixed-integer polytope reformulated with binary variables, one can apply sequential convexification: for a polytope \(Q\subseteq[0,1]^h\times\mathbb{R}^{n-h}\) and a binary variable \(y_j\), define
\[
Q^{y_j}:=\operatorname{conv}\bigl(\{q\in Q: y_j=0\}\cup\{q\in Q: y_j=1\}\bigr).
\]
Iterating this operation over selected binary variables removes vertices fractional in those variables.

Fix a natural binarization \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) of \(x\) with range \(\{0,1,\dots,d\}\) and view it as part of some binary extended formulation. For \(\alpha\in\{0,1,\dots,d-1\}\), consider the integrality-separation property for \(x\): no vertex of the current relaxation has \(\alpha< x < \alpha+1\). The paper defines a rank parameter \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) measuring the minimum number of sequential convexifications on the \(d\) binary variables \(y_1,\dots,y_d\) needed to enforce this property.

For the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) (a \(d\)-simplex with \(d+1\) vertices) and the full binarization \(B_F(d)\) (also a \(d\)-simplex), the paper derives closed-form expressions showing that \(B_U(d)\) has smaller rank than \(B_F(d)\) under this criterion.

A binarization is said to be isomorphic to a \(d\)-simplex if, as a polytope, it is combinatorially equivalent to a simplex (equivalently, it has \(d+1\) vertices and complete graph skeleton).
problem_definition_latex:
Among all natural binarizations \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) of an integer variable \(x\in\{0,1,\dots,d\}\) whose polytope \(B\) is isomorphic (combinatorially equivalent) to a \(d\)-dimensional simplex, determine whether the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) minimizes the rank parameter among this class.

Equivalently: decide whether, for every natural simplex-isomorphic binarization \(B\) of \(x\in\{0,1,\dots,d\}\) and for every \(\alpha\in\{0,1,\dots,d-1\}\), one has
\[
\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\ge \mathrm{rk}_{B_U(d)}(\alpha),
\]
or otherwise provide a counterexample binarization \(B\) (simplex-isomorphic and natural) with strictly smaller rank for at least one such \(\alpha\).
latex:
Let \(x\) be an integer variable taking values in \(\{0,1,\dots,k\}\). A (polyhedral) binarization of \(x\) is a polytope \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) in variables \((x,y)\), where \(y\in[0,1]^d\) are intended to be binary, such that
\[

Fix a natural binarization \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) of \(x\) with range \(\{0,1,\dots,d\}\) and view it as part of some binary extended formulation. For \(\alpha\in\{0,1,\dots,d-1\}\), consider the integrality-separation property for \(x\): no vertex of the current relaxation has \(\alpha< x < \alpha+1\). The paper defines a rank parameter \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) measuring the minimum number of sequential convexifications on the \(d\) binary variables \(y_1,\dots,y_d\) needed to enforce this property.

For the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) (a \(d\)-simplex with \(d+1\) vertices) and the full binarization \(B_F(d)\) (also a \(d\)-simplex), the paper derives closed-form expressions showing that \(B_U(d)\) has smaller rank than \(B_F(d)\) under this criterion.

Among all natural binarizations \(B\subseteq \mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) of an integer variable \(x\in\{0,1,\dots,d\}\) whose polytope \(B\) is isomorphic (combinatorially equivalent) to a \(d\)-dimensional simplex, determine whether the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) minimizes the rank parameter among this class.

\[
\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\ge \mathrm{rk}_{B_U(d)}(\alpha),

open_problem_quote: In light of Lemmas 4.11, 4.13, one concludes that the unary binarization \(B_U(d)\) is better than the full binarization \(B_F(d)\) in terms of rank. We do not know whether \(B_U(d)\) is optimal with respect to all binarizations that are isomorphic to a \(d\)-simplex. We leave this as our first open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The search space can be parameterized as small polytopes with \(d+1\) vertices in \(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1]^d\) satisfying the binarization conditions; for a fixed small \(d\), one can enumerate candidate vertex sets (or constraint descriptions) and compute the rank via the paper’s set-cover formulation on edge indicator vectors. Violations (a binarization beating \(B_U(d)\)) can be certified computationally by explicitly solving the associated set covering instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate binarizations can be represented as vertex descriptions or linear inequality systems with rational coefficients, with constraints enforced by feasibility checks (binarization property, naturalness, simplex combinatorics). Fitness can be the computed rank (or worst-case rank over \(\alpha\)) obtained by extracting the skeleton/edges and solving the induced set covering problems, enabling iterative improvement and discovery of counterexamples or best-in-class constructions.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem is posed in Aprile–Conforti–Di Summa, which introduces natural binarizations and a rank parameter \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) capturing how many sequential convexifications on the binary variables of a fixed binarization \(B\) are needed to eliminate vertices with \(\alpha<x<\alpha+1\). For the two canonical simplex binarizations of \(x\in\{0,\dots,d\}\)—the unary \(B_U(d)\) and full \(B_F(d)\)—the paper gives closed forms: \(\mathrm{rk}_{B_U(d)}(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_\ell)=\ell\) for distinct \(\alpha_j\), while \(\mathrm{rk}_{B_F(d)}(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_\ell)=d-\min_j\alpha_j\), implying \(B_U(d)\) dominates \(B_F(d)\) under this criterion. The rank is formulated as a set covering problem over indicator vectors of \(\alpha\)-edges in the skeleton of \(B\), so for simplex-isomorphic binarizations (complete skeleton on \(d+1\) vertices) the question becomes a combinatorial optimization problem over how the \(x\)-labels \(0,1,\dots,d\) are assigned to the simplex vertices and how the corresponding \(y\in[0,1]^d\) coordinates determine edge indicator vectors.

Beyond this source, the closest foundational work is Dash–G{"u}nl{"u}k–Hildebrand on binary extended formulations and unimodular binarizations, which evaluates binarizations via split closures rather than sequential convexification rank. Balas’ disjunctive programming results underlie the sequential convexification procedure and justify interpreting ranks as minimum hitting/set-cover numbers. Earlier computational/theoretical motivation for comparing binarizations via disjunctions appears in Owen–Mehrotra (limitations of binary expansions) and Bonami–Margot (cut generation through binarization).

No forward-citation evidence was provided, and within the supplied material the simplex-isomorphic optimality of \(B_U(d)\) is explicitly left open. Thus the problem remains open: it asks whether any other natural binarization polytope combinatorially equivalent to a simplex can yield strictly smaller \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) than unary for some \(\alpha\). Promising directions suggested by the source paper’s framework include: (i) classifying all natural simplex-isomorphic binarizations up to affine/unimodular transformations and understanding how edge indicator vectors can vary; (ii) translating the rank comparison into extremal set-cover bounds for complete graphs with edge labels induced by \(y\)-patterns; and (iii) searching for counterexamples via small \(d\) enumeration of simplex vertices in \([0,1]^d\) with integer \(x\)-labels and computing the induced set-cover ranks.

sources.title: Binary extended formulations and sequential convexification
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces natural binarizations and defines the rank parameter \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) measuring the minimum number of sequential convexifications on the binary variables needed to enforce integrality-separation for \(x\) between \(\alpha\) and \(\alpha+1\). Computes closed forms for unary \(B_U(d)\) and full \(B_F(d)\), proving \(\mathrm{rk}_{B_F(d)}(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_\ell)\ge \mathrm{rk}_{B_U(d)}(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_\ell)\) for distinct \(\alpha_j\). Leaves open whether \(B_U(d)\) is optimal among natural simplex-isomorphic binarizations.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open problem: among natural binarizations \(B\subseteq\mathbb R\times[0,1]^d\) that are combinatorially equivalent to a \(d\)-simplex, decide whether unary \(B_U(d)\) minimizes \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\) for all \(\alpha\in\{0,\dots,d-1\}\).

sources.title: Binary extended formulations of polyhedral mixed-integer sets
sources.bibtex:
@article{DashGunlukHildebrand2018BinaryExtendedFormulations,
  title   = {Binary extended formulations of polyhedral mixed-integer sets},
  author  = {Dash, Sanjeeb and G{"u}nl{"u}k, Oktay and Hildebrand, Robert},
  journal = {Mathematical Programming},
  volume  = {170},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {207--236},
  year    = {2018},
  doi     = {10.1007/s10107-017-1215-2},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1007/s10107-017-1215-2}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a theory of binary extended formulations for mixed-integer sets; introduces and studies unimodular binarizations and shows their optimality with respect to split closures; provides structural results on how binarizations interact with split disjunctions.
sources.connection: Provides the main prior framework for comparing binarizations (via split closure) and motivates studying alternative criteria (like sequential-convexification rank). While not addressing simplex-isomorphic optimality, it supplies tools/definitions (binarizations, natural/integral/exact, unimodularity) that constrain the class of candidate simplex binarizations.

sources.title: Disjunctive programming: Properties of the convex hull of feasible points
sources.bibtex:
@article{Balas1998DisjunctiveProgramming,
  title   = {Disjunctive programming: Properties of the convex hull of feasible points},
  author  = {Balas, Egon},
  journal = {Discrete Applied Mathematics},
  volume  = {89},
  number  = {1--3},
  pages   = {3--44},
  year    = {1998},
  doi     = {10.1016/S0166-218X(98)00136-5},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1016/S0166-218X(98)00136-5}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes sequential convexification (iterated convex hull of faces induced by binary disjunctions) and its convergence for polytopes in \([0,1]^h\times\mathbb R^{n-h}\).
sources.connection: Sequential convexification is the underlying procedure used to define \(\mathrm{rk}_B(\alpha)\); Balas' theorem justifies interpreting rank as a minimum hitting/set-cover problem over fractional vertices.

sources.title: On the value of binary expansions for general mixed-integer linear programs
sources.bibtex:
@article{OwenMehrotra2002ValueBinaryExpansions,
  title   = {On the value of binary expansions for general mixed-integer linear programs},
  author  = {Owen, Jonathan H. and Mehrotra, Sanjay},
  journal = {Operations Research},
  volume  = {50},
  number  = {5},
  pages   = {810--819},
  year    = {2002},
  doi     = {10.1287/opre.50.5.810.358},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1287/opre.50.5.810.358}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes binary expansion (logarithmic) reformulations in MILP; shows limitations of convexification/branching on binary expansion variables for tightening the original integer variable range.
sources.connection: Provides earlier evidence that different binarizations behave differently under disjunction-based tightening; contrasts with unary/full and motivates rank comparisons across binarizations, though not specific to simplex-isomorphic binarizations.

sources.title: Cut generation through binarization
sources.bibtex:
@article{BonamiMargot2015CutGenerationBinarization,
  title   = {Cut generation through binarization},
  author  = {Bonami, Pierre and Margot, Fran{}ois},
  journal = {Mathematical Programming},
  volume  = {154},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {197--223},
  year    = {2015},
  doi     = {10.1007/s10107-014-0826-6},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1007/s10107-014-0826-6}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies generating cutting planes via binarization-based extended formulations and disjunctions; analyzes strength and practical aspects of binarization in cut generation.
sources.connection: Contextual background on why binarization choice matters for disjunction-based strengthening; complements the rank viewpoint though does not address simplex-isomorphic optimality.
